Back to overview

Continuation patterns

Choose a short chord phrase, then compare how the observed continuations differ across the full collection and individual genres. Read this like a musician asking, "in this style, where does this harmonic thought usually go next?" The source data comes from Chordonomicon, a web-scraped Ultimate Guitar chord corpus, so guitar-based popular harmony is strongly represented.

Start here: choose 3 chords, search for a familiar shape like C G Amin, then compare global corpus with a genre. The bars show which next moves are ordinary, which are style-leaning, and how strong that tendency is.

What a musician would notice

Shared grammarSee next moves that remain common across the whole collection.
Genre accentSwitch the corpus slice to hear which paths lean more strongly toward one style.
What Hn meansH3 means a three-chord phrase, H4 means four chords, and so on.

Interpretation

Style contrast

How to read the chart

Each row keeps the end of the selected phrase and adds one chord. Longer bars mean that continuation makes up a larger share of the observed examples in the selected genre or corpus slice. Click a row to pin it.

Observed continuations

For fair comparison, phrases are transposed to begin on C. That means C G Amin represents the same chord shape no matter which key a song originally used. The corpus comes from the Chordonomicon dataset, built from Ultimate Guitar chord charts.
